network.overall_strategy: A multi-environment synthesis network converging on glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) through 10 distinct pathways. Hydrothermal environments fix CO₂ to C₁ building blocks (formate, formaldehyde, CO) on iron-sulfur minerals and drive reductive amination of glyoxylate on ferroan brucite and ethanolamine oxidation on metal catalysts. Surface environments exploit Strecker chemistry, formaldimine–formic acid coupling, UV photochemistry, and formose-derived glyoxylate pathways. Five hub molecules (NH₃, formaldehyde, formaldimine, glyoxylate, HCN) interconnect the pathways, creating a robust, redundant network grounded in literature from Miller (1953), Chimiak et al. (2024), Zhang et al. (2017), Xu et al. (2024), and Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026).

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite
reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs onto the Fe₃S₄ surface (structural analog of ferredoxin [Fe₄S₄] clusters), where H₂ dissociatively adsorbs. Hydride transfer from surface Fe-H to CO₂ produces surface-bound formate (HCOO⁻), which desorbs into alkaline vent fluid.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is a foundational reaction of the iron-sulfur world hypothesis and a plausible analog of the acetyl-CoA pathway. Experimental work by Yamaguchi et al. (2014) and Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO₂ fixation on iron-sulfur minerals at hydrothermal conditions. This provides the C₁ feedstock for downstream formaldehyde and glycine synthesis.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions in high-T vent zones

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ undergoes reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite surface: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O. Magnetite acts as a redox-active catalyst, cycling between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ states to facilitate oxygen transfer from CO₂ to H₂.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite in hydrothermal systems has been demonstrated to produce CO and longer-chain hydrocarbons (McCollom & Seewald, 2006). CO is a key building block for the direct carbonyl route to glycine described by Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026) on silicate surfaces.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Formate (HCOO⁻) adsorbs on magnetite surface; Fe⁰ or surface Fe²⁺ donates electrons to reduce the C=O to CH₂O. The reaction proceeds via a surface-bound formyl (HCO·) intermediate that is further hydrogenated to formaldehyde. H₂O is released.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of formate to formaldehyde is thermodynamically challenging but feasible under strongly reducing hydrothermal conditions with Fe⁰ as electron donor (Herschy et al., 2014). Formaldehyde is a critical C₁ hub feeding into Strecker, formose, and direct carbonyl pathways to glycine. Xu et al. (2024) highlight formaldehyde and formaldimine as key intermediates toward glycine.

reactions.name: Miller-Urey electric discharge / UV photolysis

conditions.catalyst: TiO₂ photocatalyst, UV radiation, electrical discharge
conditions.other: Anoxic early atmosphere, CH₄/NH₃/H₂O gas mixture, strong UV (no ozone layer)

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge or UV photolysis dissociates CH₄ and NH₃ to generate radical species (CH₃·, NH₂·, H·, CN·). Recombination of C and N radicals produces HCN. Partial oxidation of CH₄ by OH radicals (from H₂O photolysis) yields formaldehyde (H₂CO). TiO₂ mineral dust in the atmosphere photocatalyzes these radical reactions under UV irradiation.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953) demonstrated that electric discharge on CH₄/NH₃/H₂O produces HCN and formaldehyde as primary products, which then form glycine via Strecker synthesis. Schlesinger & Miller (1983) confirmed HCN and H₂CO as key intermediates. Cleaves et al. (2008) showed CO/N₂ atmospheres also produce these species. TiO₂ aerosols in the early atmosphere would enhance UV-driven radical chemistry (Dunn et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Aminomethanol formation from formaldehyde and ammonia

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly alkaline pH, prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of NH₃ to the carbonyl of formaldehyde produces aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH), a hemiaminal. This reaction is rapid and essentially barrierless in aqueous solution. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate both reactants through adsorption and provide a microenvironment that favors the addition.
reactions.reasoning: Aminomethanol is a well-established intermediate in computational studies of prebiotic glycine synthesis. Xu et al. (2024) identified NH₂CH₂OH as a key node in the pathway from formaldehyde and ammonia to glycine, noting its role as a precursor to the highly reactive formaldimine (CH₂=NH). The reaction is known to proceed readily in aqueous solution.

reactions.name: Dehydration of aminomethanol to formaldimine

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling; dehydration occurs during dry phase on clay surface

reactions.mechanism: During the drying phase of a wet-dry cycle, aminomethanol loses water to form formaldimine (CH₂=NH). Montmorillonite clay surface acts as a Lewis acid catalyst, activating the hydroxyl group for elimination. The dry phase drives the equilibrium toward dehydration.
reactions.reasoning: Xu et al. (2024) computationally demonstrated that dehydration of aminomethanol to formaldimine is a critical step in glycine synthesis, and identified formaldimine as 'especially reactive toward amino acids.' This dehydration is thermodynamically favored under dry conditions and represents the branching point between the Strecker pathway and the formic acid coupling pathway.

reactions.name: Strecker cyanohydrin step: formaldimine + HCN → aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Slightly acidic to neutral pH pool, re-wetting phase of wet-dry cycle

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (CN⁻) to the electrophilic carbon of formaldimine (CH₂=NH) forms aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). The imine carbon is activated by protonation under slightly acidic conditions. Montmorillonite concentrates both HCN and formaldimine on interlayer surfaces, promoting the reaction.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic Strecker synthesis step, as reviewed by Miller (1953) and many subsequent workers. The combination of formaldimine (or its imine equivalent from H₂CO + NH₃) with HCN to form aminoacetonitrile is the defining step of the Strecker route to amino acids. Xu et al. (2024) note however that on mineral surfaces the direct Strecker (HCN) step can be kinetically challenging, making the formaldimine route preferable.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: Prolonged aqueous conditions; mildly acidic pH 4-6 favors hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first the nitrile group (C≡N) is hydrated to an amide (glycinamide, NH₂CH₂CONH₂), then the amide is further hydrolyzed to glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) + NH₃. Protons from acidic mineral surfaces catalyze the hydrolysis. The released NH₃ is recycled as a hub reactant.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acid is the final step of Strecker synthesis, demonstrated in Miller's original experiments (Miller, 1953) and confirmed in numerous subsequent studies. Acid hydrolysis was typically used experimentally to convert nitrile intermediates to amino acids. Under prebiotic conditions, this hydrolysis would be slower but still feasible at elevated temperatures or on mineral surfaces (Cleaves et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Formaldimine + formic acid coupling to glycine

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite, formic acid as proton shuttle
conditions.other: Evaporitic pool setting; moderate temperature; formic acid acts as both reactant and catalyst

reactions.mechanism: Formaldimine (CH₂=NH) reacts with formic acid (HCOOH) in a concerted or stepwise mechanism: the imine carbon is attacked by the formate carboxylate oxygen, or more likely HCOOH protonates the imine nitrogen while its carboxylate attacks the α-carbon, yielding an N-formyl intermediate that rearranges/hydrolyzes to glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH). Xu et al. (2024) identified this as proceeding through an 'iminoglycine' (NH=CHCOOH) intermediate that tautomerizes to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Xu et al. (2024) computationally identified the formaldimine + formic acid pathway as having lower barriers (~40-50 kcal/mol) than classic Strecker steps on surfaces. They highlighted iminoglycine (NH=CHCOOH) as a key intermediate and formic acid as functioning both as a reactant (C₁ source) and a proton-shuttle catalyst. This represents an alternative to the Strecker route that bypasses the need for HCN.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine on ferroan brucite

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite, Fe(II) as electron donor
conditions.other: Alkaline pH 8-9, anaerobic, subsurface aquifer or cold vent setting; 100 mM glyoxylate; weeks timescale

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate (OCHCOOH) condenses with NH₃ to form an imine (glyoxylamide or glyoxylate-imine). Surface Fe(II) in ferroan brucite donates two electrons to reduce the C=N imine bond, yielding glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH). The mineral surface both adsorbs the organic intermediates and provides the reducing equivalents. Up to 97% of glycine product remains mineral-bound.
reactions.reasoning: Chimiak et al. (2024) demonstrated this reaction explicitly: glyoxylate (100 mM) mixed with NH₃ and ferroan brucite at ~20 °C yielded ~52-69% conversion to glycine over weeks. They showed that Fe(II) in brucite acts as both electron donor and adsorption surface. Notably, ~97% of glycine remained mineral-bound, detectable only after acid digestion. This is a key hydrothermal route that does not require HCN.

reactions.name: Ethanolamine oxidation to glycine at alkaline vents

conditions.other: Alkaline pH ~9.7, anaerobic, simulated Archean serpentinizing vent

reactions.mechanism: Ethanolamine (HOCH₂CH₂NH₂) undergoes metal-catalyzed dehydrogenation/oxidation. The terminal alcohol is first oxidized to an aldehyde (aminoacetaldehyde), which is then further oxidized to the carboxylic acid (glycine). Alternatively, the C-OH may be directly activated on the metal surface. Fe/Ni/Co catalysts facilitate C-H and O-H bond activation at high temperature and pH.
reactions.reasoning: Zhang et al. (2017) demonstrated that ethanolamine yields glycine efficiently under high-temperature (80-160 °C), high-pH (~9.7) conditions with Fe/Co/Ni/Cu catalysts, simulating alkaline basaltic vents. Glycine appeared 'rapidly and efficiently' within minutes to hours. This pathway provides a plausible non-HCN route to glycine in serpentinizing hydrothermal systems where ethanolamine can form from ethylene oxide and ammonia.

reactions.name: Direct carbonyl route: H₂CO + CO + NH₃ → glycine on mineral surface

conditions.other: Mineral grain surface; warm subseafloor or vent wall; single exothermic step

reactions.mechanism: On the mineral surface, NH₃ first attacks formaldehyde to form an aminomethanol or formaldimine intermediate that remains surface-bound. CO then inserts into the C-N bond (or reacts with a surface-bound amino-carbene), forming glycine in a concerted, exergonic process with a single low energy barrier. The mineral surface orients reactants and stabilizes the transition state.
reactions.reasoning: Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026) used quantum simulations on forsterite (Mg₂SiO₄) surfaces to show that H₂CO + CO + NH₃ can react to form glycine in a single-barrier, exergonic step, bypassing the kinetically blocked surface Strecker (HCN) pathway. This neutral pathway was found to be much more favorable than Strecker on mineral surfaces. Applied here to analogous Fe-bearing mineral surfaces (magnetite, pyrite) in hydrothermal settings.

reactions.name: Formose-type aldol: formaldehyde dimerization to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline evaporitic pool; borate stabilizes sugar intermediates; formose reaction conditions

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. Ca(OH)₂ generates the enolate of formaldehyde (the Cannizzaro intermediate), which attacks a second formaldehyde molecule to form glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO). Borate minerals complex with the diol product, stabilizing it against further polymerization.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction converting formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde and higher sugars is well established (Butlerow, 1861; Ricardo et al., 2004). Borate stabilization of sugar intermediates prevents tar formation. Glycolaldehyde is a key C₂ intermediate that connects hydrothermal C₁ chemistry to glyoxylate and sugar pathways. This provides the precursor for glyoxylate synthesis via oxidation.

reactions.name: UV photooxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV irradiation (no ozone layer), shallow pool, anaerobic oxidation using mineral oxidant

reactions.mechanism: TiO₂ absorbs UV light, generating electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated holes oxidize glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) at the alcohol group, converting -CH₂OH to -CHO, producing glyoxylate (OCHCOOH). Under anoxic conditions, Fe(III) minerals or surface-generated oxidants serve as the terminal electron acceptor instead of O₂.
reactions.reasoning: Photooxidation of organic molecules on TiO₂ is well documented. Glyoxylate is a central metabolic intermediate and a direct precursor to glycine via reductive amination (Chimiak et al., 2024). This reaction connects the formose-derived glycolaldehyde from surface pools to the glyoxylate reductive amination pathway in hydrothermal settings, creating a key cross-environment link.

reactions.name: Nitrate reduction to ammonia on ferroan brucite

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite, Fe(II) as electron donor
conditions.other: Alkaline pH 8-9, anaerobic, subsurface serpentinizing environment; weeks timescale

reactions.mechanism: NO₃⁻ adsorbs on the ferroan brucite surface and is stepwise reduced by Fe(II): NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ → NO → NH₂OH → NH₃. Each reduction step involves electron transfer from structural Fe(II) in the brucite lattice. The mineral is partially oxidized (Fe(II) → Fe(III)) in the process. The produced NH₃ remains partially adsorbed on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: Chimiak et al. (2024) demonstrated that ferroan brucite couples nitrate reduction to organic amination. In their NO₃⁻ experiments, nitrate was reduced to NH₃ in situ, which then reacted with glyoxylate to form glycine. Initial glycine yields were <3% (due to slow NO₃⁻ → NH₃ conversion), but nearly all product was mineral-bound (~97%). This pathway is critical for environments where free NH₃ is scarce but NOₓ species are available from atmospheric photochemistry.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal Strecker: CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde → Strecker → glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is fixed to formate on greigite, reduced to formaldehyde on magnetite, then enters the surface Strecker pathway via aminomethanol and formaldimine to produce aminoacetonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glycine. This pathway links deep hydrothermal CO₂ fixation to surface Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey atmospheric Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Methane and ammonia in the early atmosphere are dissociated by UV/discharge to produce HCN and formaldehyde. These enter the Strecker pathway on surface clay minerals: formaldehyde + ammonia → aminomethanol → formaldimine + HCN → aminoacetonitrile → glycine.

pathways.name: Formaldimine + formic acid coupling (Xu et al. pathway)
pathways.description: Formaldehyde (from hydrothermal or atmospheric sources) reacts with ammonia to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to formaldimine. Formaldimine couples with formic acid (from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction) to yield glycine via an iminoglycine intermediate, bypassing HCN entirely.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey formaldimine + formate route
pathways.description: Atmospheric H₂CO from Miller-Urey chemistry enters surface pools, forms aminomethanol → formaldimine. Meanwhile hydrothermal formate is transported to surface. Formaldimine couples with formate to yield glycine without HCN.

pathways.name: Glyoxylate reductive amination with free NH₃ (Chimiak et al.)
pathways.description: Glyoxylate (derived from glycolaldehyde photooxidation in surface pools) is transported to hydrothermal settings where it undergoes reductive amination with free NH₃ on ferroan brucite surfaces. Fe(II) provides the reducing equivalents. Up to 69% yield demonstrated.

pathways.name: Nitrate reduction + glyoxylate amination (Chimiak et al. coupled pathway)
pathways.description: In oxidizing atmospheres lacking free NH₃, nitrate is reduced to ammonia in situ on ferroan brucite. The produced NH₃ then reductively aminates glyoxylate on the same mineral surface to form glycine. Both reactions are coupled on the same mineral.

pathways.name: Ethanolamine hydrothermal oxidation (Zhang et al.)
pathways.description: Ethanolamine, formed in hydrothermal settings from ethylene oxide and ammonia, is oxidized to glycine on Fe/Ni/Co metal surfaces at alkaline pH (~9.7) and high temperature (80-160 °C). This is a single-step, efficient conversion in serpentinizing vent conditions.

pathways.name: Direct carbonyl route on mineral surface (Mates-Torres & Rimola)
pathways.description: Formaldehyde, carbon monoxide, and ammonia react directly on a mineral surface (forsterite/magnetite analog) in a single exothermic step to form glycine. This bypasses all Strecker intermediates and represents the most direct route from C₁ molecules to glycine.

pathways.name: CO₂ → CO → direct carbonyl → glycine (full hydrothermal)
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite at high temperature. CO then combines with hydrothermally-derived formaldehyde and ammonia on mineral surfaces to form glycine directly via the carbonyl insertion pathway.

pathways.name: Formose-glyoxylate-glycine surface-hydrothermal loop
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation produces formaldehyde, which is transported to surface pools where formose chemistry dimerizes it to glycolaldehyde. UV photooxidation on TiO₂ converts glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate. Glyoxylate returns to hydrothermal settings for reductive amination to glycine on ferroan brucite, completing a surface-hydrothermal loop.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Solid pathway combining hydrothermal CO₂ fixation with surface Strecker chemistry. The weakest link is rxn_003 (formate → formaldehyde reduction on magnetite), which is thermodynamically uphill and not well-demonstrated experimentally. Requires HCN from a separate branch (rxn_004), creating an inter-pathway dependency that is appropriate for a network but means P1 is not self-contained. Good environmental transition from hydrothermal to surface.
suggestions: Consider alternative formaldehyde sources or cite specific evidence for formate reduction on magnetite
Specify mineral catalyst for surface Strecker steps (e.g. montmorillonite)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic Miller-Urey to Strecker route, extremely well-supported by Miller (1953) and decades of follow-up experiments. All steps are chemically sound. The main limitation is that the entire pathway is surface-based, lacking environmental diversity. rxn_008 stoichiometry is slightly simplified (actually requires 2 H₂O), but conceptually correct. CH₄ starting material requires a reducing atmosphere assumption.
suggestions: Note that aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through an amide intermediate
Consider specifying mineral catalyst for hydrolysis step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Based on Xu et al. (2024) computational pathway. The formaldimine + formic acid coupling (rxn_009) is supported by DFT calculations showing moderate barriers (~40-50 kcal/mol), but lacks experimental validation. The hydrothermal-to-surface transition is well-motivated. Literature notes that HCOOH can act as both reactant and proton shuttle, which is mechanistically interesting but the barrier may be prohibitive without additional catalysis.
suggestions: Add mineral catalyst specification for rxn_009 to lower barrier
Cite specific barrier values from Xu et al. to justify feasibility

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Variant of P3 using Miller-Urey atmospheric source instead of hydrothermal formate. Same concern about rxn_009 lacking experimental verification. Mixing atmospheric Miller-Urey products with hydrothermal formate is environmentally complex—the formate transport to surface is not explicitly addressed. Slightly less coherent than P3 due to mixed sourcing.
suggestions: Clarify how hydrothermal formate reaches surface environment
Consider whether surface evaporite conditions could drive the coupling

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent pathway grounded in Chimiak et al. (2024). The reductive amination of glyoxylate on ferroan brucite (rxn_010) is experimentally demonstrated with ~52-69% yields. The glyoxylate sourcing via formose aldol + UV photooxidation (rxn_013, rxn_014) is plausible and creates a nice surface→hydrothermal environmental flow. NH₃ availability is assumed but justified by alkaline vent conditions. The formaldehyde dimerization to glycolaldehyde is well-known formose chemistry.
suggestions: Specify UV wavelength range for rxn_014
Note that Chimiak et al. found ~97% of glycine was mineral-bound

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Directly models the Chimiak et al. (2024) integrated pathway coupling nitrate reduction with glyoxylate amination on ferroan brucite. Experimentally validated, though yields with NO₃⁻ were lower (<3% initially) compared to free NH₃ system. The pathway elegantly addresses the nitrogen source problem for non-reducing atmospheres. Environmental flow from hydrothermal nitrate reduction to surface glyoxylate synthesis to hydrothermal amination is well-justified.
suggestions: Note the weeks-long timescale required for nitrate-derived glycine
Mention that yields improve when mineral-bound glycine is accounted for

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The ethanolamine oxidation reaction itself is well-supported by Zhang et al. (2017) at 80-160°C, pH 9.7 with Fe/Co/Ni/Cu catalysts. However, ethanolamine (mol_013) is an orphan input—no synthesis route is provided in the network. This makes the pathway incomplete. Where does ethanolamine come from prebiotically? Without addressing this, the pathway is a dangling endpoint. The reaction itself is chemically sound but the pathway lacks provenance.
suggestions: Add a synthesis route for ethanolamine (e.g., from ethylene oxide + NH₃ or from serine decarboxylation analogs)
Alternatively, reclassify ethanolamine as a starting material with justification

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Based on Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026) computational study on forsterite surfaces. Single-step, exergonic, low-barrier process. However, this is only computationally validated—no experimental confirmation. The pathway assumes all three precursors (H₂CO, CO, NH₃) are co-located on mineral surfaces. As a standalone pathway this is thin, but as part of the network (with precursors from hydrothermal reactions), it gains strength.
suggestions: Specify forsterite or similar silicate as the mineral surface
Note this is computationally predicted, not experimentally verified

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full hydrothermal pathway from CO₂ through RWGS to CO, with formaldehyde from formate reduction, converging on the direct carbonyl route. Nicely self-contained within hydrothermal environment. The RWGS on magnetite (rxn_002) is well-established. The main concerns are rxn_003 feasibility and the computational-only support for rxn_012. NH₃ source needs to come from the network (rxn_015 or rxn_004). The pathway has good environmental coherence as a purely hydrothermal route.
suggestions: Strengthen rxn_003 with specific experimental references
Note temperature/pressure requirements for each step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Surface-hydrothermal loop pathway creating glyoxylate through formose chemistry and UV oxidation, then completing with hydrothermal reductive amination. Very similar to P5 but explicitly traces from CO₂. The hydrothermal→surface→hydrothermal loop is an interesting and realistic environmental progression. Well-supported by Chimiak et al. for the final amination step. The formose aldol step is prebiotically plausible, and UV photooxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate is reasonable.
suggestions: Clarify the transport mechanism between hydrothermal and surface environments
Consider whether glycolaldehyde survives the transition

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent and comprehensive synthesis network for prebiotic glycine. The network demonstrates strong topology with 17 molecules, 15 reactions, and 5 well-chosen hub molecules (NH₃, CH₂O, HCN, glyoxylate, formaldimine) that provide genuine chemical interconnection. Ten distinct pathways offer redundancy and robustness, with 5 different final reactions producing glycine—a hallmark of a well-designed convergent network. Literature alignment is outstanding: the network explicitly incorporates routes from Miller (1953), Chimiak et al. (2024), Zhang et al. (2017), Xu et al. (2024), and Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026), covering all major known prebiotic glycine synthesis mechanisms. Environmental progression is well-handled with clear hydrothermal→surface→biochemical flows, and mineral catalysts (greigite, magnetite, ferroan brucite, forsterite) are specified for most hydrothermal reactions. The main weaknesses are: (1) ethanolamine (mol_013) is an orphan intermediate with no synthesis route, making P7 incomplete; (2) rxn_003 (formate→formaldehyde) is thermodynamically challenging and underspecified; (3) several surface reactions lack mineral catalyst specifications; (4) rxn_009 and rxn_012 are computationally supported but lack experimental validation. Despite these issues, the network achieves its target through multiple validated routes and the overall quality is high.
